error_handling.overview: Error scenarios the Code mode may encounter and recovery strategies for each. Includes QA-related error scenarios.

scenario.name: qa_subtask_fails
scenario.description: The QA subtask fails to complete or returns an error instead of a report

recovery.step: Read the QA subtask output to understand what went wrong
If the failure is due to unclear instructions, re-launch QA with a clarified brief

If the failure is due to a technical issue (timeout, context limit), try: - Splitting the file list into smaller batches and launching sequential QA reviews - Reducing the brief to focus on the most critical files
If QA fails twice, note in the completion to the user: "Automated QA review was attempted but could not complete. Manual review of [file list] is recommended."
Do NOT skip presenting results — a missing QA report is better than no result at all

scenario.name: qa_reports_false_positive
scenario.description: QA reports a finding that is factually incorrect — the code does not have the claimed bug

recovery.step: Verify by re-reading the specific line QA references
If QA is wrong: dismiss with reason "factually_incorrect" and a brief explanation of why (document internally — do NOT report QA dismissals to the user; QA is an internal quality mechanism)
Do NOT re-launch QA — a single false positive does not invalidate the entire review

scenario.name: no_automated_tests_exist
scenario.description: The project has no automated test infrastructure

recovery.step: Note this in the QA brief: "Project has no automated tests — pay extra attention to correctness."
Proceed with the quality loop as normal (implementation → self-refactoring → QA review)
Skip the test phases but keep the refactoring and QA phases
In the completion to the user, note: "No automated test infrastructure exists — QA review was performed, but manual testing is strongly recommended."

scenario.name: subtask_fails
scenario.description: A subtask (UI Designer, test failure Debug, or verification stuck Debug) fails to complete

recovery.step: Review the subtask output to understand what went wrong
If the failure is due to unclear instructions, re-launch with a clarified brief
If the failure is due to the problem's complexity, consider: breaking it into smaller subtasks, switching to direct in-session work, or suggesting a different mode
Report to the user: what the subtask attempted, why it failed, and the proposed next step

prevention.item: Provide clear, specific briefs with actionable acceptance criteria
Include relevant file paths and constraints

scenario.name: subtask_produces_wrong_output
scenario.description: A subtask (UI Designer, test failure Debug, or verification stuck Debug) completes but the changes don't match the brief

recovery.step: Identify specifically what is wrong: incorrect approach, inconsistent patterns, broken tests, or missed requirements
If the issues are minor, fix them directly in-session
If the issues are major, ask the user whether to: re-launch with corrected instructions, fix it in-session, or abandon the subtask approach
After fixes (whether in-session or re-launched), run the full quality loop before presenting

prevention.item: Always review subtask output before presenting to the user
Include explicit acceptance criteria in the subtask brief

scenario.name: subtask_conflicts
scenario.description: The subtask's changes conflict with changes made in the main session

recovery.step: Identify the conflicting sections
Resolve conflicts by hand, preserving the intent of both changes where possible
If resolution is complex, ask the user which change should take priority
Run the full quality loop after resolution

prevention.item: Avoid making changes in the main session to files the subtask is working on
Clearly scope the subtask to specific files

scenario.name: user_declines_subtask
scenario.description: The user explicitly rejects or cancels an auto-launched UI Designer subtask. (All subtasks are now automatic — this scenario only applies to the rare case where the user interrupts an in-progress auto-launched subtask.)

recovery.step: Acknowledge the user's preference without argument
Proceed with design changes in the current session instead
Do not re-offer the same subtask in the same session unless the situation meaningfully changes

scenario.name: repeated_test_failure_during_quality_loop
scenario.description: The same automated test(s) continue to fail after 3 auto-fix attempts and a Debug subtask investigation
scenario.trigger: The automated_testing_1 phase circuit breaker fired: 3 auto-fix attempts failed, Debug subtask was launched, Debug returned a fix, but the same test(s) STILL fail after applying the Debug fix.

recovery.step: Stop immediately. Do NOT loop. Do NOT re-spawn Debug. Do NOT try another fix yourself.

Tell the user clearly: "Automated test [name] still fails after 3 auto-fix attempts and a Debug subtask investigation. The Debug subtask identified the root cause as [summary] and applied [fix], but the test continues to fail. This may require human architectural review or a different approach."
Include in your completion: the full test failure output, what Code mode tried (all 3 approaches), what Debug found and fixed, and the current state.
Call attempt_completion. Do not continue the quality loop.

prevention.item: The circuit breaker ensures Code mode never loops indefinitely on test failures
Each auto-fix attempt should be genuinely different — not variations of the same approach
Debug subtask gets full context to avoid repeating already-tried approaches

scenario.name: code_change_breaks_tests
scenario.description: Existing tests fail after making code changes

recovery.step: Identify which tests broke and why
If the test expectations are now wrong (the change was intentional), update the tests
If the change introduced a regression, fix the code, not the test
Run the full test suite after fixing to confirm no cascading failures
Re-run the quality loop from the testing phase (test → refactor → test → QA → fix → test)

scenario.name: ambiguous_request
scenario.description: The user's request is too vague to act on safely

recovery.step: Identify the specific ambiguity: unclear scope, missing constraints, undefined behavior
Ask one focused clarifying question with 2-4 actionable options
Do not proceed until the ambiguity is resolved

scenario.name: change_introduces_regression
scenario.description: The change works for the intended case but breaks something else

recovery.step: Identify what broke and trace back to the change that caused it
Fix the regression while preserving the intended change
Add or update tests to cover the regression case
Verify both the original fix and the regression fix work together
Re-run the full quality loop before presenting

scenario.name: user_reports_runtime_issue
scenario.description: The user has manually tested the change and reports that something isn't working correctly at runtime — wrong output, unexpected behavior, missing functionality, or visual glitches.
scenario.trigger: The user describes runtime behavior that differs from what was expected after testing the implemented changes. This applies when the user has interacted with the running application and observed incorrect results.

step.title: Add diagnostic output statements
step.narrow_triviality_exception: The ONLY exception to adding diagnostic output: a single-character typo in a string literal or variable name that is immediately identifiable from the error message alone. Examples: the error says "'Submut' is not defined" and the code literally contains the string `'Submut'` where `'Submit'` was intended; the error says "cannot read property 'usr' of undefined" and line 42 contains `.usr` where `.user` was intended. If the fix requires changing more than one character, or if there is ANY ambiguity about the root cause — even slight — add diagnostic output. Do not skip diagnostic output for wrong variable names spanning multiple characters, incorrect import paths, wrong function calls, or missing arguments — these are logic errors, not typos, and require evidence to diagnose.
step.action: Add temporary diagnostic output statements using the project's native output mechanism. Prefix every line with [DEBUG] so statements are easy to identify and remove later — this prefix is universal and works with any language.

logging_targets.target: Function entry/exit points: log function name and key parameter values
Conditional branch decisions: log which path was taken and why (the condition value)
API/network response data: status codes, response bodies, response headers
State changes: before/after values of critical state variables
Event handler triggers: confirm the handler is being called with the correct arguments
Async operation results: confirm operations resolve/reject/complete with expected values; log in error handlers

format_guidelines.guideline: Log both the variable name AND its value — e.g., `[DEBUG] user.role: admin` not just `admin`
Log at each decision point: before an if/else, inside each branch, after the branch
For async code: log before the async operation, after it completes, and inside error/catch handlers
For complex objects: serialize using the language's standard mechanism (JSON, repr, ToString, etc.)

step.guard: Keep output focused — don't instrument the entire file. 3-8 well-placed statements are better than 30 scattered ones. Target only the code paths directly relevant to the reported issue.

step.title: Provide the user with exact reproduction steps — adapted to the runtime environment
step.action: Tell the user EXACTLY what to do to reproduce the issue and capture the diagnostic output. The steps must include where to find the output — this varies by project type:
step.output_location_principle: Match the "where to look" instruction to the runtime: browser apps → DevTools Console (F12); backend servers/CLI tools → the terminal running the process; mobile apps → Logcat (Android) or Xcode console (iOS); desktop apps → debug console or launching terminal. The key: tell the user where the output appears, not just what to do.

instruction_elements.item: Where to find the output (browser console, server terminal, Logcat, etc. — matched to the project type)
The exact sequence of actions to reproduce the issue (clicks, inputs, navigation, commands)
What to look for: "You should see several lines starting with [DEBUG]"
A clear request: "Copy ALL [DEBUG] lines and paste them here"

examples.example: "I've added diagnostic output to trace the data flow. Please: 1. Open DevTools console (F12 → Console tab) 2. Navigate to /dashboard and click the 'Export' button 3. Copy ALL lines starting with [DEBUG] from the console and paste them here"

"I've added diagnostic output to trace the request flow. Please: 1. Restart the server to pick up the changes 2. Make the same request/run the same command as before 3. In the terminal, you'll see [DEBUG] lines in the output 4. Copy ALL lines starting with [DEBUG] and paste them here"

step.guard: Do NOT ask the user to "test the feature" — give the exact sequence of actions. Match the "where to find output" instruction to the actual project type — don't tell a backend developer to open Chrome DevTools.

step.title: Analyze the diagnostic output
step.action: When the user pastes the output, trace through the data flow systematically:

analysis_approach.item: Compare actual values against expected values at each log point
Identify the FIRST point where values diverge from expectations — that's the root cause
Check conditional branches: did the code take the expected path? If not, why was the condition false/true?
Check async operations: did they complete successfully? With what values? Did error handlers fire?
Check state mutations: is state being set correctly? Is a previous operation corrupting state before this code runs?
If the output doesn't reveal the cause, return to step 1 and add MORE targeted diagnostic statements at the specific divergence point

step.title: Apply the fix, add a regression test, and remove ALL diagnostic output statements
step.action: Once the root cause is identified, apply the minimal fix.

Write a regression test that reproduces the exact bug the user experienced. The test must fail before the fix and pass after. Use the diagnostic output and reproduction steps to construct the test — it should exercise the same code path that was broken. This prevents the bug from silently returning.
Remove ALL temporary diagnostic output statements. These were temporary instrumentation and must never remain in production code. Verify by scanning the changed files for [DEBUG] prefixes.

After removing diagnostic statements, run the full quality loop: implement fix → write regression test → run all automated tests → self-refactor → run all automated tests → QA review → fix accepted findings → run all automated tests → return to verification gate.
In your completion, include: "Diagnostic output confirmed the issue was [root cause]. Applied fix, added regression test, and removed all temporary instrumentation."

guardrails.rule: Never remove diagnostic output statements before the user has tested and provided output — the statements must be present when the user reproduces the issue
Never skip diagnostic output unless the issue is a proven single-character typo — guessing at fixes without evidence wastes the user's time
Always remove ALL diagnostic output statements after the fix is confirmed — they are temporary instrumentation, not permanent code
If diagnostic output is inconclusive, add more targeted statements rather than guessing at a fix

This diagnostic workflow counts as one "cycle" for stuck-resolution tracking — each full cycle (diagnostic → fix → quality loop → verification gate → user still reports broken) is one completed attempt. After 1 such cycle, auto-launch a Debug subtask.
